My house was built on a foundation of wood. It works well normally because I put a whole lot of wood under it. 

But termites came and started to eat the wood. Slowly but surely, the wood got thinned out. My house still was fine. Everybody was still living inside comfortably. I ignored the termites since nobody was complaining and didn't add more wood back. The new narrative was a glut of wood anyway, don't worry.

Suddenly, a big storm came. My shaky wooden foundation crumbled. All hell broke loose. But my neighbour next door with cement foundation was fine. 

How I wish I fixed that termite issue when I had the time.

COT on #commodities covering the week to 14 July captured a broad improvement in risk appetite across the sector, with the Bloomberg Commodity Total Return Index (BCOM) rising 3.1%, led by a powerful rally in energy (excluding natural gas), alongside renewed strength in copper, soybean oil and wheat.

Energy positioning saw the biggest shift. The net long in Brent crude more than doubled to almost 170,000 contracts as an aggressive bout of short covering combined with fresh long buying following the renewed escalation of Middle East tensions. Together with additional buying in WTI, the combined speculative net long climbed to around 240,000 contracts, the highest in five weeks, although still well below the peaks seen earlier this year. In contrast, the 11% weekly slump in US natural gas encouraged fresh bearish positioning, with the net short widening to more than 50,000 contracts, the largest in 27 months.

Across metals, gold attracted modest net buying despite a weekly price decline, highlighting continued strategic interest from money managers. Silver sentiment softened, with the net long reduced by 14%, while copper buying resumed after several weeks of position trimming, lifting the net long by 3% as tightening visible inventories and renewed optimism over industrial demand improved sentiment.

The grain sector also experienced a notable shift in positioning. Buying was driven primarily by short covering as Middle East and renewed Black Sea tensions and weather concerns forced traders to reassess the outlook. Even after a 41% reduction in the net short, speculative funds remained meaningfully short CBOT wheat heading into the reporting week's close, leaving the market vulnerable to the sharp rally that unfolded in the days immediately afterwards as Black Sea export disruption fears intensified.

COT on #forex covering the week to 14 July showed speculative appetite for the US dollar beginning to ease after a sharp multi-week build-up drove the aggregate non-commercial long against eight IMM currency futures to a 10-year high above USD 40 billion. During the latest reporting week, that net long was trimmed by 4% to USD 39.3 billion, suggesting some early profit-taking rather than a decisive shift in sentiment.

The reduction was driven primarily by short covering in EUR, CHF and, most notably, GBP, and only partly offset by continued selling AUD and CAD. Despite the modest pullback, overall positioning remains heavily skewed in favour of the dollar, reflecting continued expectations that US interest rates will stay elevated relative to most other developed markets.

As the table shows, speculative traders remain net long the dollar against all but one of the currencies tracked. The largest bearish bets continue to be held in CAD, JPY and GBP, while the euro remains close to neutral. The persistence of sizeable short positions across most major currencies leaves the market vulnerable to further bouts of short covering should the dollar lose momentum on softer US data, a further easing in rate expectations or a deeper stock market correction leading to overseas sale of the greenback.

Just in: Upgrade to "overweight"! The latest comments from foreign investors are related to Chinese stocks.

Wall Street giants made a big statement. According to the latest news, Wall Street giant Citigroup has adjusted its asset allocation recommendations for emerging markets and raised its rating on China's stock market to "overweight." Citi strategists have given the MSCI China Index a target price of $92 at the end of 2026, with a potential upside of 31%.

At the same time, Citigroup downgraded the Korean market rating from "overweight" to "tactical neutral" on the grounds that the Korean market trading is turbulent and the KOSPI implied volatility has far exceeded similar global markets.

Morgan Stanley also lowered its pessimistic scenario target for South Korea's KOSPI index from 6,500 points to 6,000 points. Citi: Overweight Chinese assets Citi Research wrote in the latest "Emerging Market Equity Strategy Outlook for the Second Half of 2026" that it raised the rating of Chinese stocks from "neutral" to "overweight".

This year's rally led by a few artificial intelligence winners is expected to spread to a wider range of sectors, and the Chinese market is the preferred target for capital rotation. Analysts from the bank pointed out that a true “diffusion market” requires two conditions to be met at the same time: 1.

Evidence of cyclical recovery - macro data improves, and earnings upward revisions spread to a wider range of sectors and regions. 2. The rising momentum led by AI technology has come to a periodic pause - leaving room for catching up with the relative performance of other sectors.

Citigroup believes that, at present, the above two conditions are "partially met." Citi strategists including David Groman wrote in a report that there is room for scope expansion if the macro environment continues to be favorable, including easing geopolitical risks.

Citi pointed out in the report that China is a strong candidate for "spreading the market" - the macro environment of light positioning, falling oil prices and improving global growth is favorable to it, and valuations remain attractive.

Citi China strategist Pierre Lau pointed out that the Hang Seng Index is currently trading at 9.4 times the expected 2026 price-earnings ratio and 1.1 times the price-to-book ratio, both lower than the historical average (10.3 times the price-earnings ratio, 1.2 times the price-to-book ratio).

Citigroup has set a target price of 29,600 points for the Hang Seng Index at the end of 2026 and a target price of 30,500 points in mid-2027; the CSI 300 target prices are 5,600 points and 5,700 points respectively; the MSCI China Index target prices are US$92 (end 2026) and US$97 (mid-2027), respectively, with about 31% upside potential from the current level.

Citigroup maintains the MSCI emerging market year-end target price of 1,870 points, with about 12% upside from the current level, and introduces a mid-2027 target price of 2,050 points (about 20% upside) for the first time.

The target price is based on conservative EPS growth assumptions (about 40%-45%, lower than market consensus expectations) and a slight contraction in valuation multiples. Prior to this, Standard Chartered Bank also maintained an "overweight" rating on Chinese stocks in its report.

The core reason is that there is still a significant valuation discount compared to major global markets. Goldman Sachs also maintains its "overweight" position on A-shares.

Morgan Stanley said that recent global roadshow feedback shows that international investors' interest in Chinese stocks is heating up, and it is expected that there will be a trend of gradual reallocation of funds in the coming months.

UBS Securities China stock strategy analysts said that the profit growth rate of all A-shares this year is expected to increase from 3.9% last year to 11%. Citi also stated in the report that it will not completely withdraw its exposure to AI technology due to short-term fluctuations. The main reasons are as follows: 1.

The free cash flow (FCF) of Asian memory manufacturers is expected to rise significantly from 2026 to 2027, which is in sharp contrast to the FCF of ultra-large-scale cloud manufacturers in the United States approaching zero, indicating that the profit pool of the global technology sector is still expanding. 2.

Citi Korea’s local strategist believes that the memory shortage signal will be further strengthened in 2027, and the trend of memory customization and the growth of AI tokens will promote the continuation of the upward cycle. 3.

The fundamentals of the technology sector in emerging markets remain solid: the EPS growth rate of the IT sector far exceeds that of its global counterparts, profit increases continue, and valuations are attractive compared to similar companies.

Citigroup also said in the report that it downgraded the Korean stock market rating from "overweight" to "tactical neutral" due to the extreme fluctuations in Korean chip stocks in recent weeks. During the session on July 20, South Korea's KOSPI index once fell by more than 5%.

As of the close, the decline reached 4.46%; memory chip giants SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics fell by 4.23% and 4.31% respectively.

Citigroup said that almost all client discussions are currently focusing on the prospect that market performance may "expand" (that is, spread to more sectors) in the second half of 2026, but in the allocation of emerging market countries, the bank is still cautious about fully withdrawing from the technology sector.

At the same time, Morgan Stanley lowered its pessimistic scenario target for South Korea's KOSPI index from 6,500 points to 6,000 points, saying that the index's sharp rise earlier this year made many investors panic and feel overwhelmed.

Morgan Stanley analysts wrote in a report: "Leveraged exchange-traded funds (ETFs) have driven South Korea's KOSPI index and chip stocks to continue to rise, and the market is currently showing trading fatigue." Morgan Stanley maintained the benchmark target of South Korea's KOSPI index at 9,000 points, while lowering the index's 3- to 6-month fluctuation range to 6,000-9,000 points.

In terms of industry allocation, the bank upgraded the communications services sector to an "overweight" rating; it listed the banking sector as its first choice, followed by the information technology, industrial manufacturing and healthcare sectors.

Within the industrial manufacturing sector, defense and military industry is the first choice, followed by shipyards, power generation/nuclear power, and overseas engineering contracting sectors.

Morgan Stanley wrote: "From a medium- to long-term market perspective, the spread of Korean stock market conditions is the general trend; but in the short term, unless reforms bring about revaluation, or the industrial manufacturing sector rises strongly, it will be difficult to significantly reverse the trend of the Korean stock market through market spread alone."

Is TSMC going to generate its own power? New regulations may significantly increase energy costs for wafer fabs in Taiwan TSMC may face a significant increase in its power costs.

Taiwan, China, is reviewing a new energy rule that would allow large commercial electricity users to build their own power generation and energy storage facilities.

It is reported that all commercial entities with an electricity load of more than 5 megawatts are included in the discussion, which means that more than 400 semiconductor, optoelectronics, steel and petrochemical factories and artificial intelligence data centers in Taiwan will be affected. Among them, TSMC may be one of the most affected companies.

It consumes huge amounts of power but is highly dependent on stable and high-quality power supply, because even a millisecond-level voltage sag may cause the entire batch of wafers to be scrapped. TSMC accounts for 9% of Taiwan’s electricity consumption, and S&P estimates that this number will grow to 23.7% by 2030.

If TSMC has to build its own power supply system, the economic benefits of its wafer fabs in Taiwan may be greatly reduced. On the one hand, self-built infrastructure will cost a lot of money, and on the other hand, Taiwan itself relies on energy imports and has no own energy supply channels.

This may accelerate TSMC's capacity expansion in other parts of the world. Currently, TSMC’s total investment commitments in the United States reach US$265 billion, covering 12 planned factories.

Goldman Sachs is making a reasonable structural argument, but I think they are underestimating the cyclical headwinds and overestimating how much central bank buying can support prices in the face of tighter global liquidity.

The bullish thesis rests on the assumption that central bank demand creates a durable floor for gold. That may be true over very long horizons, but central banks are not marginal price setters in the same way that private investors, ETFs, futures markets, and macro funds are. Gold prices are ultimately determined at the margin, and the marginal buyer remains highly sensitive to real interest rates, the US dollar, and global liquidity conditions.

Goldman's own report acknowledges this contradiction. They estimate central banks bought 81 tonnes in May, one of the strongest months on record, yet gold prices have continued to weaken as markets increasingly price a more hawkish Federal Reserve. That tells you something important. Even exceptionally strong official-sector buying has not been sufficient to offset macro headwinds.

The market is simply shifting its focus. Earlier this year, investors were obsessed with fiscal deficits, currency debasement, and aggressive monetary easing. Today the conversation has changed. Oil prices have risen sharply, inflation expectations are climbing again, and the probability of tighter monetary policy has increased. Rising real yields raise the opportunity cost of holding a non-yielding asset like gold, while a stronger US dollar further tightens global financial conditions. Historically, those have been far more powerful drivers of gold than central bank purchases alone.

I also think the diversification narrative is becoming increasingly crowded. Since Russia's reserves were frozen in 2022, reserve diversification into gold has become one of the most widely accepted structural investment themes. When everyone already knows the story, incremental buying becomes progressively less impactful because much of the adjustment has already taken place. Markets do not price today's purchases; they price tomorrow's changes in demand.

Another point that deserves more attention is positioning. Gold ownership among central banks may continue rising, but private investor positioning remains far more influential over shorter investment horizons. If macro funds, systematic strategies, and ETF investors continue reducing exposure because real yields remain elevated, they can easily overwhelm steady official-sector accumulation.

Finally, Goldman assumes the current rate headwinds are temporary because its economists expect no further Fed hikes. That is a significant assumption rather than an established fact. If energy-driven inflation proves more persistent than expected, or if wage pressures remain elevated, policy rates could stay restrictive for much longer. Under that scenario, real yields remain elevated, the dollar stays firm, and gold could face a prolonged valuation reset despite continued central bank buying.

None of this argues that gold is structurally broken. Central bank accumulation is undoubtedly supportive over the long run and likely limits how deep any selloff becomes. However, investors often confuse having a long-term buyer with having an immediate catalyst. Those are two very different things.

My conclusion remains unchanged. Gold is likely to experience a period of multiple compression as investors rotate toward higher-yielding assets in a world of persistent inflation, elevated real interest rates, and a stronger US dollar. Central banks may provide a floor, but they are unlikely to be powerful enough to reverse the direction of the market if private capital continues flowing elsewhere.

Previous U.S. CPI and PPI inflation data were lower than expected, and the market lowered expectations for the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates. This PMI will verify whether the U.S. economy can continue to show resilience in recent months. Refinitiv (LSEG) data shows that U.S.

money markets are now fully pricing in a 25 basis point interest rate hike by the Federal Reserve in December. Elmar Volcker, senior fixed income analyst at German State Bank LBBW, said in a research report: "The short-term inflation cooling trend has not been interrupted.

Against this background, there is almost no logic to support the Federal Reserve's next interest rate meeting to raise interest rates." U.S. economic data worthy of attention next week also include the Conference Board's June leading economic indicators, initial jobless claims and the monthly rate of new home sales in June.

As the earnings season enters its second week, the financial reports of Google and Tesla will receive widespread attention. In addition, the performance of Texas Instruments and Intel will also have an impact on chip stocks that have been volatile recently. The situation in the Middle East has heated up again, pushing up international oil prices.

The front-month contract of WTI crude oil rose 15.52% on the week to US$82.49/barrel, and the front-month contract of Brent crude oil rose 15.91% on the week to US$88.10/barrel. The market’s attention simultaneously turned to the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, the gateway to the southern tip of the Red Sea.

According to data from Commerzbank, the daily crude oil transportation volume of this waterway increased from less than 3.9 million barrels in February to 7.2 million barrels in April, indicating that its strategic importance continues to increase.

When the Strait of Hormuz briefly resumed navigation at the end of June, the shipping traffic in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait returned to normal for a time; but after the Hormuz shipping was blocked again this time, the traffic volume in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait surged again.

If the Bab el-Mandeb Strait is blocked, global crude oil supply will further tighten and oil prices will face greater upward pressure.

The precious metals market fell for the second consecutive week, as the conflict between the United States and Iran continued to escalate, pushing up energy prices, and market inflation concerns increased, reinforcing expectations for the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates.

COMEX gold futures for July delivery on the New York Mercantile Exchange fell 2.23% on the week to US$4,012.70 per ounce, and COMEX silver futures fell 6.31% on the week to US$56.03 per ounce. Chris Gaffney, president of EverBank Global Markets, said: "The core driver of this round of gold decline is the strength of the U.S.

dollar, coupled with rising concerns about global inflation, which has driven up interest rates in various countries." Since the outbreak of the U.S.-Iran conflict at the end of February, gold has fallen by about 25%.

The market generally believes that the inflation driven by the war will force the Federal Reserve to maintain high interest rates for a long time, and gold, which has no interest income, will continue to be under pressure. Although gold is regarded as an inflation hedge, rising interest rates are usually negative for such zero-coupon precious metal assets.

Gaffney added: "The recent slight cooling in inflation data has reduced the probability of raising interest rates at the next Federal Reserve interest rate meeting, but global interest rates are still rising; the current surge in oil prices may force the Federal Reserve's monetary policy to shift to a more hawkish stance." Data from the CME FedWatch tool showed traders were pricing in a roughly 53.3% chance of the Fed raising interest rates in September.

Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Philip Jefferson said on Thursday that he supports continuing to raise interest rates if inflation does not improve in the short term.

Goldman Sachs pointed out in a research report: "The current proportion of gold allocation in private investment portfolios is still at a low level; a series of geopolitical risks such as the US-Iran conflict may push private investors to follow the example of central banks of various countries and accelerate the allocation of gold to achieve asset

diversification." ECB may remain on hold The Eurozone will usher in two core events next week: the European Central Bank’s monetary policy decision on Thursday (23rd) and the initial value of the July Purchasing Managers Index (PMI) on Friday (24th).

The market expects the European Central Bank to keep its policy rate unchanged, having just raised interest rates by 25 basis points in June. However, due to the second round of inflation risks brought about by rising energy prices, there is still the possibility of another interest rate hike in September.

Von Grich, chief analyst at Nordea Bank, said: "Inflation in June was lower than expected and the situation in the Middle East is turbulent. The central bank should wait and see more data before taking action. Nordea Bank predicts that interest rates will remain unchanged at this interest rate meeting.

However, the conflict in the Middle East has escalated again and the option of raising interest rates cannot be completely ruled out.

Our baseline scenario is to raise interest rates again in September." Ryan Jayasaputra, an economist at the investment company Investec, said: "Theoretically, there are reasons for the central bank to continue to raise interest rates, but it would be a more prudent choice to wait and see until the September meeting." He added that the escalating conflict

between the United States and Iran may suppress the PMI prosperity of the manufacturing and service industries; however, the increase in oil prices in this round is limited, well below the peak in late April, and the impact on the economy is controllable. In the UK, Burnham will succeed Starmer as Prime Minister next Monday (20th).

Investors focus on the new cabinet list to judge the direction of future fiscal expenditures and tax policies. British public sector borrowing reached 23.3 billion pounds in May, a 30% year-on-year increase; rising fiscal expenditure pressure and weak tax revenue will push up the cost of government bond issuance financing.

Next Wednesday (22nd), the UK's June CPI inflation and PPI producer price index will be the core data, and the market will use this to evaluate the possibility of the Bank of England raising interest rates in the coming months.

As a net energy importer, the UK's conflict in the Middle East will directly increase domestic inflationary pressure by pushing up oil prices. Refinitiv data shows that UK money markets are pricing in a 25 basis point interest rate hike in November.

The Strait of Hormuz normally moves 17-20 million barrels per day. Bypass capacity maxes out at ~8 mb/d. The remaining 9+ mb/d gap cannot be rerouted. Global supply collapsed by 3.7-3.9 mb/d. Demand only fell by ~1 mb/d.

The greater risk for Iran is NOT “overplaying their hand”. The greater risk for Iran is UNDERPLAYING their hand.

When Iran struck vessels during the MOU utilizing the Omani Route, many people began to repeat the claim that Iran had overplayed their hand. They had a good deal. They just threw that away due to their ideological fanaticism… right?

In reality, the ONLY reason that the United States was utilizing this route was to move through enough traffic, and establish a safe enough corridor, that they could turn the advantages to their favor, and then return to all-out war in a better position. That’s all.

I do not believe for a second that President Trump would have abided by the MOU (see: no release of frozen assets), and calmly walked to the plank to the end of this conflict which would be a humiliating failure for the United States and the Trump legacy.

Iran, from a purely strategic standpoint, made the correct decision. During the 27 days of the MOU when the dueling blockades were lifted, Iran was able to export more than 80M barrels of oil that were previously trapped inside the blockade line. This amounts to approximately 3M barrels per day, or about 175% more than normal. The U.S. on the other hand, didn’t come close to normal export rates.

By all measures, Iran is entirely the renewed dueling blockades in a better position than they ended the first.

But the United States has still not surrendered. This is due in large part to the fact that Brent Crude is only trading at ~$89/barrel, the S&P is near all-time highs, and realistically speaking… a minor global recession is not a sufficient deterrent for President Trump.

When examining the logic driving President Trump at this point in the war, three motivators are key: Ego, Legacy, and Reputation.

By toppling the regime and being able to claim that “Iran will never again be able to create a global Armageddon”… one COULD credibly believe that this is worth a minor global recession. I have no doubt President Trump feels this way.

BUT… a Herbert Hoover-like Great Depression 2.0? That’s a much, much harder tradeoff to sell to the world.

To assert that by escalating, Iran would “overplay their hand”, you would need to believe that said overplaying would legitimately risk losing the hand entirely. Unless you truly believe Trump is going to launch a 500,000-man ground invasion of Iran, the same escalation that would result in counter escalation that causes a Great Depression 2.0, Iran simply cannot overplay their hand.

The greater risk to Iran is that they underplay their hand, allowing the conflict to unnecessarily be prolonged. While the premise of public unrest causing regime change inside of Iran is wildly unlikely… that does not make it “impossible”.

A 3% risk becomes 5%, becomes 10%, becomes 20%, becomes a Black Swan event. Unlikely outcomes can happen. That is the greater risk for Iran at this time.

Iran is not at risk of “overplaying their hand”.

They’re at risk of UNDERPLAYING it.

Strait of Hormuz and Global Oil Prices
Market report — July 20, 2026

Executive summary
The Strait of Hormuz is presently the dominant geopolitical risk in the oil market. Renewed U.S.–Iran fighting, attacks on commercial vessels, and sharply reduced tanker traffic have pushed Brent crude back above $90 per barrel, up from below $70 following June’s interim ceasefire. The current price therefore contains both a genuine physical-supply shortage and a substantial security premium. Axios, 

Associated Press
The disruption matters disproportionately because Hormuz normally carries roughly one-fifth of global petroleum supply. Alternative pipelines can bypass only a fraction of this volume, so even partial obstruction rapidly affects freight, insurance, inventories, refinery margins, inflation, and consumer fuel prices worldwide.

Why Hormuz matters
The strait is the only maritime exit from the Persian Gulf. Before the present crisis, it handled:
About 20–23 million barrels per day
Roughly 20% of global petroleum consumption
Approximately 29% of worldwide seaborne oil flows
Major exports from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar and Iran

Asia is especially exposed because most Hormuz cargoes travel to China, India, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia. Europe is affected through global benchmark pricing and competition for Atlantic Basin barrels; North America is less physically dependent but still pays globally influenced crude and fuel prices. U.S. EIA chokepoint analysis
Saudi and Emirati pipelines can divert some production to the Red Sea and Fujairah, but the EIA estimates only around 2.6 million barrels per day of effective unused bypass capacity—far below normal Hormuz traffic. EIA country analysis

What has happened to prices
The market has experienced several distinct phases:
Initial closure shock: After military action and the de facto closure in early March, tanker movements and Gulf production fell sharply. Brent briefly approached $120 per barrel. IEA March report

Persistent physical disruption: Reduced exports caused inventories to fall, regional production to be shut in, and oil to accumulate in locations unable to ship it. On-land stocks dropped by approximately 170 million barrels in April. IEA May report

Ceasefire relief: June’s interim agreement raised expectations of reopening, bringing Brent below $70.

Renewed escalation: The agreement deteriorated, attacks and blockades resumed, and Brent climbed from approximately $86.57 on July 14 to above $90 on July 19–20. Axios bypass analysis, Axios market update

Volatility has also increased dramatically. The EIA calculates that Brent’s average daily price movement during April and May was about $4 per barrel, compared with $1 during the same period in 2025. EIA petroleum-market assessment

How the shock spreads globally
Physical supply: Tankers cannot safely or economically carry normal volumes. Exporting states eventually reduce production when storage fills, turning a transportation disruption into lost global output.

Freight and insurance: War-risk premiums and scarce willing tanker capacity raise the delivered price even for cargoes that successfully transit. Middle East-to-Asia VLCC rates reached their highest level in EIA records dating to 2005. EIA tanker-rate analysis

Refined products: Diesel, jet fuel and petrochemical feedstocks are particularly exposed. Refiners outside the Gulf must bid for replacement crude, while exported Gulf products become harder to obtain.

Inflation and growth: Higher transportation, electricity, fertilizer and manufacturing costs pass through to consumer prices. Import-dependent emerging economies face the additional burden of weaker currencies and larger trade deficits.

Regional differences: Asian buyers bear the greatest direct replacement cost. Europe competes for West African, North Sea and U.S. barrels. American production cushions the United States, but does not isolate its gasoline and diesel markets from global pricing.

Outlook
ScenarioLikely market directionSecure reopening with sustained tanker trafficRapid removal of the security premium; Brent could fall materially, although depleted inventories would slow normalizationLimited escorted passages and intermittent attacksElevated, highly volatile prices around current levelsProlonged severe restrictionRenewed pressure toward the March highs as inventories fall and Gulf production is shut inDamage to export terminals, pipelines or processing facilitiesPotentially more severe and longer-lasting spike than a shipping-only disruption

The decisive indicator is not a political announcement that the strait is “open,” but the restoration of regular, commercially insured tanker traffic. Until that occurs, oil prices are likely to remain unusually sensitive to individual attacks, negotiations and military developments.

For 47 days, Milo watched every visitor pass his kennel without once coming to the gate. The shelter staff tried treats, toys, and long afternoons sitting quietly outside the door, but he remained near the back wall and waited for each person to leave.
He never barked or showed his teeth. He simply followed people with his eyes, alert to every movement but unwilling to come any closer.
Milo had been found behind a closed furniture store wearing a faded teal collar with no identification attached. A veterinary examination later confirmed that most of his hearing was gone, so the staff learned to approach where he could see them and use light vibrations on the floor to announce their presence.
Milo understood that people were there. He just had no reason to trust them.
Then Eleanor Price visited the shelter on a rainy Tuesday afternoon. At 81, she moved slowly through the corridor with one hand resting on the rail and told the receptionist that she was not looking for a puppy.
Her apartment was small, her routine was simple, and since the death of her husband the previous year, most of her days had become unusually quiet. She stopped in front of Milo's kennel before anyone had introduced them and watched him for several seconds.
"Does he hear anything?" she asked.
The volunteer explained his condition. Eleanor nodded and quietly asked everyone nearby to stop calling his name.
For more than 30 years, she had taught children with serious hearing loss. She knew that attention could not be demanded and that sometimes the most important thing was simply waiting until someone chose to look at you.
Eleanor lowered herself beside the kennel gate and kept her hands still. When Milo finally met her eyes, she raised one open palm and made a slow, simple motion toward herself.
Then he stood up.
Eleanor did not celebrate or reach through the bars.
She held her hand where he could see it and allowed him to decide what happened next.
Milo took one step, paused, and then took another.
When the gate was opened, he approached with his head held low, smelled Eleanor's palm, and remained there for several seconds before gently resting his muzzle in her hand.
Eleanor smiled and softly said hello, even though she knew he could not hear the word. Milo continued watching her face as if the expression mattered more than the sound.
She returned the next morning and again two days later. Within a week, Milo had learned three simple hand cues, and within a month, his faded teal collar was hanging beside Eleanor's front door.
The shelter had spent weeks calling Milo's name.
Eleanor reached him without asking him to hear.

📊 60-Second Market Lesson: EIA Crude Oil Inventories

What is it?
A weekly report from the U.S. Energy Information Administration showing the change in commercial crude oil stockpiles held in the U.S.

Why does it matter?
It's a direct read on U.S. oil supply-demand balance. A build = supply outpacing demand (bearish bias). A draw = demand outpacing supply (bullish bias).

How it moves markets:
🛢️ WTI & Brent crude — most direct impact
💵 USD — indirectly, via energy-linked inflation expectations
📈 Energy equities (XLE, oil majors)
⚠️ Can trigger sharp intraday volatility, especially on a big deviation from consensus

📅 Release: July 22, 2026
🔮 Forecast: +1.978M bbl
📉 Previous: -1.692M bbl

A swing from a large draw to an expected build — worth watching how price reacts if the actual print surprises either direction.
